docs.title: Feature Engineering Portfolio
docs.text:
A practical collection of notes, experiments, and case studies focused on Feature Engineering for Machine Learning.

This project documents the process of discovering, evaluating, and creating predictive features through a combination of theory, experimentation, and real-world applications.

docs.title: What You'll Find
docs.text: This portfolio is organized as a progressive learning journey:

docs.title: Machine Learning Foundations
docs.text:
Understand the core concepts behind predictive modeling and the role of features in machine learning systems.

Topics include:

  • What is Feature Engineering?
  • Feature–Target Relationships
  • Baseline Models
  • Experimental Design

docs.title: Feature Discovery
docs.text:
Learn how to identify variables that contain predictive signal before creating new features.

Techniques covered:

  • Correlation Analysis
  • Mutual Information
  • Random Forest Feature Importance
  • Feature Ranking

docs.title: Feature Creation
docs.text:
Explore techniques for generating new predictive variables from existing data.

Examples include:

  • Ratios
  • Feature Interactions
  • Difference Features
  • Binning
  • Aggregations

docs.title: Practical Case Study
docs.text:
Apply feature engineering techniques to a real-world machine learning problem.

Current project:

House Prices Prediction

Techniques applied:

  • Feature Discovery
  • Feature Creation
  • Mutual Information Analysis
  • Feature Evaluation

docs.title: Learning Roadmap
docs.text:
Machine Learning Foundations
        ↓
Feature Discovery
        ↓
Feature Creation
        ↓
Practical Case Study

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
Every experiment follows the same methodology:

  1. Understand the problem.
  2. Explore the data.
  3. Establish a baseline.
  4. Generate hypotheses.
  5. Create or evaluate features.
  6. Measure impact.
  7. Document results.

docs.title: Language
docs.text: • Python

docs.title: Libraries
docs.text:
• Pandas
  • NumPy
  • Scikit-Learn
  • Matplotlib
  • Seaborn

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• Jupyter Notebook
  • Git
  • GitHub
  • MkDocs Material

docs.title: Explore the Documentation
docs.text: Use the navigation menu on the left to explore each module and follow the complete Feature Engineering workflow from theory to practical application.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text:
Before creating new features or performing Feature Engineering, it is essential to identify which variables already contain useful information for the model.

This module presents three classic approaches for evaluating feature relevance:

  • Correlation Analysis
  • Mutual Information
  • Tree-Based Feature Importance

docs.title: 1. Correlation Analysis
docs.text: Correlation measures the linear relationship between variables.

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text:
• Easy to interpret
  • Very fast to compute
  • Detects redundancy between variables

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• Only captures linear relationships
  • May overlook complex relationships

docs.title: Typical Use Cases
docs.text:
• Remove highly correlated variables
  • Identify variables related to the target

docs.title: 2. Mutual Information (MI)
docs.text:
Mutual Information measures how much information a variable provides about the target.

Unlike correlation, it:

  • Detects both linear and non-linear relationships
  • Works for both classification and regression tasks
  • Makes no assumptions about data distribution

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text:
• MI = 0 → the feature provides no information
  • High MI → the feature is relevant

docs.title: 3. Tree-Based Feature Importance
docs.text: Tree-based models (Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM) automatically compute the importance of each variable.

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text:
• Capture complex relationships
  • Account for interactions between variables
  • Robust to feature scaling

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• May favor variables with many unique values
  • Importance does not imply causality

docs.title: Recommended Workflow
docs.text:
1. Review correlations
  2. Compute Mutual Information
  3. Train a Random Forest model
  4. Compare feature rankings
  5. Remove irrelevant variables
  6. Perform Feature Engineering only on promising variables

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text:
Feature Discovery helps reduce noise, simplify models, and accelerate the feature engineering process.

Before creating new features, it is advisable to understand which existing variables already contain sufficient predictive signal.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Before creating new features, applying transformations, or experimenting with different machine learning techniques, it is essential to establish a baseline.

A baseline model provides a reference point against which all future improvements can be measured.

Without a baseline, it is impossible to determine whether a newly created feature genuinely improves model performance or simply adds complexity.

For this reason, building a baseline model is considered a fundamental step in any machine learning project.

docs.title: What is a Baseline Model?
docs.text:
A baseline model is the initial version of a machine learning model trained using the available data with minimal modifications.

Its purpose is not to achieve the highest possible performance.

Instead, its purpose is to establish a benchmark that future experiments must surpass.

The baseline represents the current level of performance before any feature engineering or optimization is applied.

docs.title: Why Baselines Matter
docs.text:
Feature engineering is an experimental process.

Every new feature represents a hypothesis about the data.

Without a baseline, there is no objective way to evaluate whether a hypothesis is correct.

A baseline allows us to:

  • Measure improvements.
  • Detect performance degradation.
  • Compare experiments fairly.
  • Avoid unnecessary complexity.
  • Quantify the impact of new features.

docs.title: Baselines and Feature Engineering
docs.text:
The primary objective of feature engineering is to improve predictive performance.

To determine whether a feature is useful, we compare model performance before and after introducing the feature.

The process follows a simple cycle:

Build Baseline
        ↓
Create Feature
        ↓
Train New Model
        ↓
Compare Results
        ↓
Keep or Remove Feature

Only features that provide measurable improvements should be retained.

docs.title: Characteristics of a Good Baseline
docs.text: A good baseline should be:

docs.title: Simple
docs.text:
The model should be easy to train and interpret.

The purpose is to create a reference point, not a highly optimized solution.

docs.title: Reproducible
docs.text:
The experiment should be repeatable.

Random seeds, validation procedures, and preprocessing steps should be documented.

docs.title: Stable
docs.text: Performance should remain relatively consistent across different validation folds or data splits.

docs.title: Relevant
docs.text: The baseline should use the same evaluation metric that will be used throughout the project.

docs.title: Building a Baseline Model
docs.text: A typical baseline construction process consists of the following steps.

docs.title: Step 1: Define the Target
docs.text:
Clearly identify the variable to be predicted.

Examples:

  • House price
  • Customer churn
  • Credit default
  • Product demand

docs.title: Step 2: Select Initial Features
docs.text:
Use the original dataset with minimal transformations.

Avoid extensive feature engineering at this stage.

The objective is to measure the predictive power of the raw data.

docs.title: Step 3: Select a Model
docs.text:
Choose a reasonable model appropriate for the problem.

Examples:

docs.title: Regression
docs.text:
• Linear Regression
  • Random Forest Regressor
  • Gradient Boosting Regressor

docs.title: Classification
docs.text:
• Logistic Regression
  • Random Forest Classifier
  • Gradient Boosting Classifier

The exact algorithm is often less important than having a consistent reference point.

docs.title: Step 4: Define a Validation Strategy
docs.text:
The validation procedure determines how performance will be measured.

Common approaches include:

docs.title: Train-Test Split
docs.text:
Simple and fast.

Suitable for initial experiments.

docs.title: Cross Validation
docs.text:
Provides more reliable estimates.

Reduces dependence on a single data split.

Often preferred in feature engineering workflows.

docs.title: Time Series Validation
docs.text:
Used when observations have temporal order.

Prevents future information from leaking into training data.

docs.title: Step 5: Measure Performance
docs.text:
Evaluate the model using an appropriate metric.

This score becomes the baseline.

docs.title: Evaluation Metrics
docs.text: The evaluation metric depends on the problem type.

docs.title: Mean Absolute Error (MAE)
docs.text:
Measures the average absolute prediction error.

Advantages:

  • Easy to interpret.
  • Robust to outliers.

docs.title: Mean Squared Error (MSE)
docs.text:
Measures average squared error.

Advantages:

  • Penalizes large mistakes more heavily.

docs.title: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)
docs.text:
Square root of MSE.

Advantages:

  • Expressed in the original target units.

docs.title: R² Score
docs.text:
Measures how much target variance is explained by the model.

Range:

  • 1 = perfect prediction
  • 0 = no improvement over the mean prediction

docs.title: Accuracy
docs.text: Percentage of correctly classified observations.

docs.title: Precision
docs.text: Measures prediction quality among positive predictions.

docs.title: Recall
docs.text: Measures how many true positives were identified.

docs.title: F1 Score
docs.text: Balances precision and recall.

docs.title: ROC-AUC
docs.text: Measures ranking quality across classification thresholds.

docs.title: Comparing Experiments
docs.text:
Every feature engineering experiment should be compared against the same baseline.

Example:

Experiment MAE Baseline 10.42 Feature A 9.87 Feature B 10.65 Feature C 9.31

Interpretation:

  • Feature A improves performance.
  • Feature B hurts performance.
  • Feature C provides the largest improvement.

This process allows objective decision-making.

docs.title: No Baseline
docs.text: Without a baseline, performance improvements cannot be measured.

docs.title: Changing Multiple Things at Once
docs.text:
If several features are added simultaneously, it becomes difficult to identify which feature caused the improvement.

Whenever possible, test features incrementally.

docs.title: Using Different Validation Strategies
docs.text:
Changing the validation procedure between experiments invalidates comparisons.

The validation methodology should remain consistent.

docs.title: Data Leakage
docs.text:
Using information unavailable at prediction time can artificially inflate performance.

A feature that improves performance because of leakage is not useful in production.

docs.title: Feature Engineering as a Scientific Process
docs.text:
Feature engineering should be approached as a sequence of experiments.

Each feature represents a hypothesis.

For example:

"This ratio may contain information relevant to the target."

The hypothesis is tested by:

  1. Creating the feature.
  2. Retraining the model.
  3. Comparing against the baseline.

If performance improves, the hypothesis gains support.

If performance does not improve, the feature should be reconsidered or discarded.

docs.title: Practical Workflow
docs.text:
A typical feature engineering workflow follows this structure:

Raw Dataset
        ↓
Baseline Model
        ↓
Feature Hypothesis
        ↓
Feature Creation
        ↓
Model Retraining
        ↓
Performance Comparison
        ↓
Feature Selection

This cycle is repeated throughout the project.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• A baseline model is the reference point for all future experiments.
  • Every feature should be evaluated relative to the baseline.
  • Improvements must be measured using a consistent validation strategy.
  • Feature engineering should be treated as an experimental process.
  • The objective is not to create more features, but to create features that improve measurable performance.
  • Without a baseline, it is impossible to know whether feature engineering is actually working.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
The ultimate goal of a machine learning model is to learn relationships between input variables (features) and the target variable.

A feature only becomes useful when it provides information that helps explain, predict, or describe the target.

Understanding the relationship between features and the target is one of the most important principles in feature engineering because every transformation, combination, or encoding is ultimately designed to improve this relationship.

docs.title: What Makes a Feature Useful?
docs.text:
Not all variables in a dataset contribute equally to a prediction task.

A useful feature is one that contains information about the target variable.

If changes in a feature are associated with changes in the target, then the feature may help a model make better predictions.

Conversely, a feature that contains little or no information about the target will contribute little value and may even introduce noise.

docs.title: Information and Predictive Signal
docs.text:
Machine learning models learn from patterns contained in the data.

These patterns are often referred to as signal.

Signal represents the useful information that helps explain or predict the target.

Noise represents random variation that does not contribute meaningful information.

The objective of feature engineering is to increase signal and reduce noise.

docs.title: Signal
docs.text:
Signal refers to any information that consistently relates to the target variable.

Characteristics of signal:

  • Improves predictions.
  • Generalizes to unseen data.
  • Reflects real-world relationships.
  • Remains relatively stable over time.

docs.title: Noise
docs.text:
Noise refers to information that appears random or unrelated to the prediction task.

Characteristics of noise:

  • Does not improve predictions.
  • Creates instability.
  • Increases overfitting risk.
  • Reduces model generalization.

docs.title: Relationships Between Features and Targets
docs.text:
Relationships can take different forms.

Understanding these forms is critical because different algorithms learn different types of relationships.

docs.title: Linear Relationships
docs.text:
A linear relationship exists when changes in a feature produce proportional changes in the target.

Characteristics:

  • Simple to model.
  • Easy to interpret.
  • Well suited for linear algorithms.

Examples include:

  • Revenue increasing proportionally with units sold.
  • Salary increasing with years of experience.

docs.title: Non-Linear Relationships
docs.text:
Many real-world relationships are not linear.

In these cases, the effect of a feature on the target changes depending on the feature's value.

Characteristics:

  • More complex patterns.
  • Often require transformations or flexible algorithms.
  • Common in real-world datasets.

Examples include:

  • Growth curves.
  • Diminishing returns.
  • Threshold effects.
  • Exponential relationships.

docs.title: The Learning Capacity of Machine Learning Models
docs.text:
Different machine learning algorithms have different abilities to learn relationships.

A feature that works well for one model may not work as well for another.

docs.title: Linear Models
docs.text:
Examples:

  • Linear Regression
  • Logistic Regression

Characteristics:

  • Naturally learn linear relationships.
  • Easy to interpret.
  • Limited ability to capture complex interactions.

Because of these limitations, feature engineering is especially important when using linear models.

Transformations can make complex relationships easier to learn.

docs.title: Tree-Based Models
docs.text:
Examples:

  • Decision Trees
  • Random Forest
  • Gradient Boosting
  • XGBoost

Characteristics:

  • Learn non-linear relationships naturally.
  • Capture interactions between variables.
  • Require less manual feature engineering.

However, explicit feature creation can still improve performance when important relationships are difficult to discover automatically.

docs.title: Neural Networks
docs.text:
Examples:

  • Deep Neural Networks
  • Multilayer Perceptrons

Characteristics:

  • Learn highly complex patterns.
  • Automatically build internal representations.
  • Require large amounts of data.

Feature engineering remains useful because better input representations often simplify learning and improve convergence.

docs.title: Why Feature Engineering Works
docs.text:
Feature engineering works because it changes how information is represented.

A transformation can expose patterns that were previously hidden.

Instead of asking the model to discover a difficult relationship on its own, feature engineering can make that relationship more explicit.

This often allows simpler models to achieve better performance.

docs.title: Feature Engineering as an Extension of the Model
docs.text:
A useful way to think about feature engineering is that every transformation becomes part of the model.

The algorithm itself does not change.

What changes is the representation of the data.

By modifying the representation, we effectively increase the model's ability to learn useful relationships.

In this sense, feature engineering extends the expressive power of a machine learning system.

docs.title: Matching Features to Models
docs.text:
An important principle is that features should be designed with the chosen model in mind.

Different algorithms benefit from different feature representations.

docs.title: Linear Models Prefer
docs.text:
• Linear relationships
  • Scaled variables
  • Normalized distributions

docs.title: Tree Models Prefer
docs.text:
• Informative splits
  • Explicit counts
  • Meaningful ratios
  • Aggregated information

docs.title: Neural Networks Prefer
docs.text:
• Scaled features
  • Stable distributions
  • Dense representations

Feature engineering should always consider the strengths and weaknesses of the target algorithm.

docs.title: The Goal of Feature Engineering
docs.text:
The purpose of feature engineering is not simply to create more variables.

The purpose is to create variables that make the relationship between features and target easier to learn.

A newly created feature is only valuable if it improves the model's ability to capture useful information.

More features do not necessarily mean better performance.

The quality of a feature is always more important than the quantity of features.

docs.title: Practical Implications
docs.text:
When designing new features, several questions should always be considered:

  • Does this feature contain information related to the target?
  • Is this information already present elsewhere?
  • Can the model learn this relationship directly?
  • Would a transformation make the relationship easier to learn?
  • Does the feature improve validation performance?

These questions form the foundation of effective feature engineering.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• A feature is useful only if it contains information related to the target.
  • Machine learning models learn relationships between features and targets.
  • Different algorithms learn different types of relationships.
  • Signal improves prediction while noise harms performance.
  • Feature engineering changes the representation of data.
  • Transformations can expose relationships that are difficult for a model to learn directly.
  • Effective feature engineering focuses on improving learnable relationships rather than simply creating more variables.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Feature Engineering is the process of creating, transforming, selecting, or combining variables (features) in order to make data more suitable for a machine learning problem.

It is one of the most important stages in the machine learning workflow because the quality of the features often has a greater impact on model performance than the choice of algorithm itself.

The purpose of feature engineering is to expose useful information contained in the data so that a machine learning model can learn patterns more effectively.

docs.title: Why is Feature Engineering Important?
docs.text:
Machine learning models can only learn relationships that are represented in the input data.

If relevant information is hidden, poorly represented, or distributed across multiple variables, the model may struggle to learn it.

Feature engineering helps reveal this information by transforming the original data into representations that are easier for the model to understand.

In many real-world problems, performance improvements obtained through feature engineering are often larger than those achieved by switching from one machine learning algorithm to another.

docs.title: Objectives of Feature Engineering
docs.text: Feature engineering is usually performed for one or more of the following reasons.

docs.title: Improve Predictive Performance
docs.text:
The most common goal is to increase the predictive power of a model.

New features can expose relationships that were not obvious in the original dataset, allowing the model to make more accurate predictions.

docs.title: Reduce Data Requirements
docs.text:
Well-designed features can make patterns easier to learn.

As a result, a model may require fewer training examples to achieve good performance.

docs.title: Reduce Computational Complexity
docs.text:
Some transformations can simplify the information contained in multiple variables.

This may reduce training time, memory consumption, or model complexity.

docs.title: Improve Interpretability
docs.text:
Feature engineering can create variables that are easier for humans to understand and reason about.

Interpretability is especially important in domains such as finance, healthcare, and business analytics.

docs.title: The Role of Feature Engineering in Machine Learning
docs.text:
Feature engineering is part of the data preparation stage that takes place before model training.

A typical machine learning workflow looks like this:

Problem Definition
        ↓
Data Collection
        ↓
Data Cleaning
        ↓
Feature Engineering
        ↓
Feature Selection
        ↓
Model Training
        ↓
Model Evaluation
        ↓
Deployment

Feature engineering acts as the bridge between raw data and machine learning algorithms.

docs.title: The Fundamental Principle of Feature Engineering
docs.text:
A feature is only useful if it contains information related to the target variable and if the model is capable of learning that relationship.

Different machine learning algorithms have different learning capabilities.

For example:

  • Linear models learn linear relationships naturally.
  • Tree-based models can learn complex non-linear patterns.
  • Neural networks can learn highly complex representations when sufficient data is available.

Because of these differences, feature engineering should always take into account the strengths and limitations of the chosen model.

docs.title: Feature Engineering as an Extension of the Model
docs.text:
A useful way to think about feature engineering is that every transformation becomes part of the model itself.

When a transformation is applied to a variable, the representation of the data changes.

This new representation may expose patterns that were difficult or impossible for the original model to learn.

In practice, feature engineering increases the expressive power of a machine learning system without changing the algorithm.

docs.title: Types of Feature Engineering
docs.text: Feature engineering techniques can generally be divided into several categories.

docs.title: Feature Creation
docs.text:
Creating entirely new variables from existing information.

Examples include:

  • Ratios
  • Mathematical combinations
  • Aggregations
  • Counts

docs.title: Feature Transformation
docs.text:
Modifying existing variables to improve their usefulness.

Examples include:

  • Logarithmic transformations
  • Scaling
  • Normalization
  • Standardization

docs.title: Feature Extraction
docs.text:
Generating new variables that summarize information from multiple features.

Examples include:

  • Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
  • Clustering-based features

docs.title: Feature Encoding
docs.text:
Transforming categorical variables into numerical representations.

Examples include:

  • One-Hot Encoding
  • Label Encoding
  • Target Encoding

docs.title: Characteristics of Good Features
docs.text:
A good feature typically satisfies several of the following conditions:

  • Contains useful information about the target.
  • Improves predictive performance.
  • Reduces noise.
  • Is stable across different datasets.
  • Generalizes well to unseen data.
  • Is understandable and interpretable.

Not every generated feature will be useful.

Feature engineering is therefore an experimental process that requires testing and validation.

docs.title: Feature Engineering as an Iterative Process
docs.text:
Feature engineering should not be viewed as a single step.

Instead, it is an iterative cycle:

Generate Hypothesis
        ↓
Create Feature
        ↓
Train Model
        ↓
Evaluate Performance
        ↓
Keep or Discard Feature
        ↓
Repeat

Each newly created feature should be evaluated against a baseline model to determine whether it provides measurable value.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Feature engineering is the process of improving data representations for machine learning.
  • The objective is to make relevant information easier for models to learn.
  • Better features often have a greater impact than more complex algorithms.
  • Every feature transformation effectively becomes part of the model.
  • Feature engineering includes feature creation, transformation, extraction, and encoding.
  • The process is iterative and should always be validated experimentally.

docs.title: Application in Practice
docs.text:
In a typical machine learning project, feature engineering involves:

  1. Understanding the problem domain.
  2. Studying the available variables.
  3. Formulating hypotheses about useful information.
  4. Creating new features.
  5. Evaluating their impact on model performance.
  6. Keeping only the features that improve the baseline.

The remainder of this repository explores the most important feature engineering techniques and demonstrates how they can be applied to real-world predictive problems.

docs.title: House Prices - Feature Engineering Project

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score, KFold, train_test_split
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error
from sklearn.feature_selection import mutual_info_regression
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

from pathlib import Path
import numpy as np import pandas as pd import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import seaborn as sns from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score, KFold, train_test_split from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error from sklearn.feature_selection import mutual_info_regression from sklearn.cluster import KMeans from sklearn.decomposition import PCA from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler from pathlib import Path In [3]: Copied!

# sqft_living = sqft_above + sqft_basement already
# So sqft_living + sqft_basement double-counts the basement.
# total built area = sqft_above + sqft_basement (same as sqft_living)
# More useful -> ratio of living area to lot size

X_trainf["TotalArea"] = X_trainf["sqft_above"] + X_trainf["sqft_basement"]
X_trainf["LivingToLot"] = X_trainf["sqft_living"] / (X_trainf["sqft_lot"] + 1)
X_trainf["RoomsPerSqft"] = (X_trainf["bedrooms"] + X_trainf["bathrooms"]) / (X_trainf["sqft_living"] + 1)
# sqft_living = sqft_above + sqft_basement already # So sqft_living + sqft_basement double-counts the basement. # total built area = sqft_above + sqft_basement (same as sqft_living) # More useful -> ratio of living area to lot size X_trainf["TotalArea"] = X_trainf["sqft_above"] + X_trainf["sqft_basement"] X_trainf["LivingToLot"] = X_trainf["sqft_living"] / (X_trainf["sqft_lot"] + 1) X_trainf["RoomsPerSqft"] = (X_trainf["bedrooms"] + X_trainf["bathrooms"]) / (X_trainf["sqft_living"] + 1) In [22]: Copied!

Log transformation so variables are not dominated by outliers.

In [23]: Copied! 
# Apply log1p transformation to right-skewed features
# yr_renovated removed -> years don't benefit from log: handled below as binary feature

skewed = ["sqft_lot", "sqft_lot15", "view", "bedrooms"]

for col in skewed:
    X_trainf[col] = np.log1p(X_trainf[col])

# yr_renovated: semantically correct encoding
X_trainf["was_renovated"] = (X_trainf["yr_renovated"] > 0).astype(int)
X_trainf = X_trainf.drop(columns=["yr_renovated"])
# Apply log1p transformation to right-skewed features # yr_renovated removed -> years don't benefit from log: handled below as binary feature skewed = ["sqft_lot", "sqft_lot15", "view", "bedrooms"] for col in skewed: X_trainf[col] = np.log1p(X_trainf[col]) # yr_renovated: semantically correct encoding X_trainf["was_renovated"] = (X_trainf["yr_renovated"] > 0).astype(int) X_trainf = X_trainf.drop(columns=["yr_renovated"]) In [24]: Copied! 
cluster_features = ["lat", "long", "sqft_living", "grade"]
cluster_features = ["lat", "long", "sqft_living", "grade"]

We scale variables to mean=0 and std=1 before clustering. K-Means uses Euclidean distance, so scale is critical.

In [25]: Copied! 
# Scale for KMeans

scaler = StandardScaler()
X_cluster = scaler.fit_transform(X_trainf[cluster_features])
# Scale for KMeans scaler = StandardScaler() X_cluster = scaler.fit_transform(X_trainf[cluster_features])

K-Means is used as a structure discovery tool, not as a predictive model. The cluster label is a new feature that summarizes location-size-quality patterns.

In [26]: Copied! 
# KMeans clustering
# FIX: keep Cluster as int (not category) so select_dtypes(include=np.number) includes it

kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=5, random_state=1, n_init=10)
X_trainf["Cluster"] = kmeans.fit_predict(X_cluster)
# KMeans clustering # FIX: keep Cluster as int (not category) so select_dtypes(include=np.number) includes it kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=5, random_state=1, n_init=10) X_trainf["Cluster"] = kmeans.fit_predict(X_cluster) In [27]: Copied! 
pca_features = ["sqft_living", "sqft_above", "sqft_basement", "bathrooms", "grade"]
pca_features = ["sqft_living", "sqft_above", "sqft_basement", "bathrooms", "grade"] In [28]: Copied! 
# Scale pca_features before applying PCA 

pca_scaler = StandardScaler()
scaled = pca_scaler.fit_transform(X_trainf[pca_features])

# Fit PCA — keep all components to inspect explained variance
pca = PCA()
components = pca.fit_transform(scaled)
# Scale pca_features before applying PCA pca_scaler = StandardScaler() scaled = pca_scaler.fit_transform(X_trainf[pca_features]) # Fit PCA — keep all components to inspect explained variance pca = PCA() components = pca.fit_transform(scaled) In [29]: Copied! 
# Add PCA components to X_trainf

for i in range(components.shape[1]):
    X_trainf[f"PC{i+1}"] = components[:, i]
# Add PCA components to X_trainf for i in range(components.shape[1]): X_trainf[f"PC{i+1}"] = components[:, i] In [30]: Copied!

docs.title: Objective¶
docs.text:
Predict house sale prices using advanced feature engineering techniques.

This notebook applies:

  • Baseline modeling
  • Feature discovery
  • Feature creation
  • Feature transformation
  • Clustering
  • PCA
  • Target Encoding
  • Model evaluation

Dataset: House Prices - Advanced Regression Techniques

docs.title: Feature Creation¶
docs.text: We have many variables representing house size — we will combine them to increase predictive power.

docs.title: Clustering Features¶
docs.text: Clustering adds a variable that summarizes 'type of house/area', helping the model capture global patterns.

docs.title: PCA¶
docs.text: PCA creates new components that are linear combinations of the original variables, retaining maximum variance.

docs.title: Baseline vs Final Comparison¶
docs.text: Both RMSEs are now in the same scale (log-transformed target), so the comparison is valid.

docs.title: Conclusions¶
docs.text:
The feature engineering process improved model performance compared with the baseline.

Techniques applied:

  • Mutual Information (feature discovery)
  • Domain-driven Features (TotalArea, LivingToLot, RoomsPerSqft, was_renovated)
  • Log Transformations (skewed features + target)
  • K-Means Clustering (geospatial + quality segmentation)
  • PCA (dimensionality reduction on correlated size features)

Future work:

  • Target Encoding for zipcode
  • Advanced Feature Selection (RFE, SHAP)
  • Gradient Boosting Models (XGBoost, LightGBM)
  • Ensemble Methods

docs.title: Project Overview
docs.text:
This document describes the feature engineering workflow applied to the House Prices prediction problem.

The objective is to improve model performance by creating, transforming and selecting features that better capture the relationship between property characteristics and sale price.

The process follows the methodology defined in this repository:

Baseline Model
      ↓
Feature Discovery
      ↓
Feature Creation
      ↓
Feature Transformation
      ↓
Feature Selection
      ↓
Model Evaluation

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Predict the final sale price of a residential property using information describing its characteristics.

docs.title: Prediction Type
docs.text: Regression

docs.title: Target Variable
docs.text: SalePrice

docs.title: Baseline Model
docs.text: Before creating new features, a baseline model was trained using the original dataset.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Establish a reference score against which all future improvements can be compared.

docs.title: Baseline Features
docs.text: All original variables after basic preprocessing.

docs.title: Baseline Validation Strategy
docs.text: Cross Validation

docs.title: Baseline Metric
docs.text: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)

docs.title: Baseline Result
docs.text: Model Score Baseline TBD

docs.title: Feature Discovery
docs.text: The first step consisted of identifying variables with potential predictive value.

docs.title: Domain Knowledge
docs.text:
Real estate knowledge was used to understand which property characteristics may influence price.

Examples include:

  • Property size
  • Property quality
  • Location
  • Age
  • Condition

docs.title: Data Visualization
docs.text:
Visual inspection was used to identify:

  • Non-linear relationships
  • Outliers
  • Skewed distributions
  • Potential feature interactions

docs.title: Mutual Information
docs.text:
Mutual Information was used to estimate the predictive relevance of individual variables.

The highest scoring features were prioritized for further engineering.

docs.title: Feature Creation
docs.text: Several new features were generated from existing variables.

docs.title: Ratio Features
docs.text: Created to represent relationships between property measurements.

docs.title: Motivation
docs.text: Ratios often capture information that absolute values cannot represent directly.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Area proportions
  • Relative property characteristics
  • Efficiency-related measurements

docs.title: Aggregation Features
docs.text: Multiple variables were combined into summary indicators.

docs.title: Motivation
docs.text: Provide the model with higher-level information.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Total living area
  • Total usable space
  • Combined quality indicators

docs.title: Count Features
docs.text: Counts were created to summarize groups of related variables.

docs.title: Motivation
docs.text: Tree-based models often benefit from explicit counts.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Number of rooms
  • Number of amenities
  • Number of property features

docs.title: Interaction Features
docs.text: Features were combined to represent interactions between important variables.

docs.title: Motivation
docs.text: Capture relationships not represented by individual variables.

docs.title: Feature Transformation
docs.text: Several transformations were evaluated.

docs.title: Log Transformations
docs.text: Applied to skewed numerical variables.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Reduce skewness and stabilize variance.

docs.title: Scaling
docs.text: Evaluated where required by specific algorithms.

docs.title: Techniques
docs.text:
• Standardization
  • Normalization

docs.title: Handling Outliers
docs.text: Extreme observations were investigated and treated when necessary.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Reduce distortion of model training.

docs.title: Categorical Feature Engineering
docs.text: Categorical variables required special treatment.

docs.title: One-Hot Encoding
docs.text: Used for low-cardinality categorical variables.

docs.title: Target Encoding
docs.text: Evaluated for high-cardinality variables.

docs.title: Motivation
docs.text: Capture the relationship between categories and sale price.

docs.title: Unsupervised Feature Engineering
docs.text: Additional features were generated using unsupervised learning techniques.

docs.title: Clustering
docs.text: K-Means clustering was explored to identify groups of similar properties.

docs.title: Potential Benefits
docs.text:
• Property segmentation
  • Location-related grouping
  • Market segmentation

docs.title: Principal Component Analysis
docs.text: PCA was investigated to identify major axes of variation.

docs.title: Potential Benefits
docs.text:
• Dimensionality reduction
  • Noise reduction
  • Discovery of hidden relationships

docs.title: Feature Evaluation
docs.text: Every new feature was evaluated using the same validation procedure as the baseline.

docs.title: Evaluation Process
docs.text:
Create Feature
      ↓
Train Model
      ↓
Cross Validation
      ↓
Compare with Baseline
      ↓
Keep or Discard

Features that improved validation performance were retained.

Features that did not improve performance were removed.

docs.title: Final Feature Set
docs.text:
The final model used a combination of:

  • Original variables
  • Engineered numerical features
  • Encoded categorical features
  • Aggregated variables
  • Interaction variables
  • Selected unsupervised features

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
The feature engineering process revealed several important insights:

  • Some simple features provided larger gains than complex transformations.
  • Ratios and aggregations often exposed useful information.
  • High-cardinality categoricals benefited from advanced encoding techniques.
  • Not every engineered feature improved performance.
  • Consistent validation was essential for reliable conclusions.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Future improvements may include:

  • Additional domain-driven features
  • Advanced target encoding techniques
  • Feature selection methods
  • Ensemble-specific feature engineering
  • Automated feature generation experiments

docs.title: Project Summary
docs.text:
The objective of this project was to improve house price prediction performance through feature engineering techniques.

A baseline model was first established using the original dataset. New features were then created, transformed and evaluated through a systematic experimentation process.

All experiments were evaluated using the same validation strategy to ensure fair comparisons.

docs.title: Metric Used
docs.text: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)

docs.title: Validation Strategy
docs.text: 5-Fold Cross Validation

docs.title: Baseline Performance
docs.text:
Model RMSE Baseline Model TBD

The baseline model was trained using the original feature set with minimal preprocessing.

This score served as the reference point for all subsequent experiments.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
The project produced several important observations:

  • Feature engineering improved model performance beyond the baseline.
  • Aggregated features provided meaningful gains.
  • Log transformations improved stability.
  • Some simple features outperformed more complex transformations.
  • Not every engineered feature was beneficial.
  • Consistent validation was essential for reliable conclusions.

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
This project reinforced several feature engineering principles:

  • Domain knowledge remains extremely valuable.
  • Simple features can have significant predictive power.
  • Feature engineering should be hypothesis-driven.
  • Every feature must justify its existence through measurable improvement.
  • A strong experimental framework is more important than generating large numbers of features.

docs.title: Future Improvements
docs.text:
Potential future work includes:

  • Advanced target encoding
  • Feature selection techniques
  • Gradient boosting models
  • Hyperparameter optimization
  • Ensemble methods
  • Automated feature generation

docs.title: Final Conclusion
docs.text:
Feature engineering successfully improved predictive performance on the House Prices dataset.

The project demonstrated how domain knowledge, feature creation, transformations, clustering and dimensionality reduction can be combined into a structured workflow for building stronger machine learning models.

The final solution outperformed the baseline while providing a deeper understanding of the relationships present in the data.
